section.title: The Linen Belt
section.sectionRationale: Jeremiah is sent on two strange errands, burying a fine linen loincloth until it rots, and watching wineskins smashed, both acted parables of Judah's ruined pride. The chapter ends with a piercing question: can a leopard change its spots? Judah's sin has become so ingrained that only God's own intervention, not self-effort, can address it.
section.thesis: Judah was meant to cling to the LORD as intimately as a loincloth clings to the waist, for His renown and praise and glory, but pride and habitual sin have ruined that closeness so completely that Judah is now as useless as a rotted garment, and as unable to change on its own as a leopard is to change its spots.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the linen loincloth buried and ruined, a sign act of Judah's spoiled closeness to God; the wineskins that will be smashed together, a picture of coming drunken judgment; the call to give glory to the LORD before darkness falls, extended even to the king and queen mother; and the piercing question, can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard its spots, exposing sin too ingrained for self-reform.

units.label: Buy yourself a linen loincloth
units.note: a sign act of ruined closeness to God

verses.text: This is what the LORD said to me: “Go and buy yourself a linen {{ezor|loincloth}} and put it around your waist, but do not let it touch water.”
So I bought a loincloth as the LORD had instructed me, and I put it around my waist.
Then the word of the LORD came to me a second time:
“Take the loincloth that you bought and are wearing, and go at once to Perath and hide it there in a crevice of the rocks.”
So I went and hid it at Perath, as the LORD had commanded me.
Many days later the LORD said to me, “Arise, go to Perath, and get the loincloth that I commanded you to hide there.”
So I went to Perath and dug up the loincloth, and I took it from the place where I had hidden it. But now it was ruined, of no use at all.
Then the word of the LORD came to me:
“This is what the LORD says: In the same way I will ruin the {{gaon|pride}} of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem.

These evil people, who refuse to listen to My words, who follow the stubbornness of their own hearts, and who go after other gods to serve and worship them, they will be like this loincloth, of no use at all.

For just as a loincloth {{davaq|clings}} to a man’s waist, so I have made the whole house of Israel and the whole house of Judah cling to Me, declares the LORD, so that they might be My people for My renown and praise and glory. But they did not listen.

Therefore you are to tell them that this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘Every wineskin shall be filled with wine.’ 
And when they reply, ‘Don’t we surely know that every wineskin should be filled with wine?’

then you are to tell them that this is what the LORD says: ‘I am going to fill with drunkenness all who live in this land, the kings who sit on David’s throne, the priests, the prophets, and all the people of Jerusalem.
I will smash them against one another, fathers and sons alike, declares the LORD. I will allow no mercy or pity or compassion to keep Me from destroying them.’”

units.label: Give glory to the LORD before He brings darkness

verses.text: Listen and give heed. Do not be arrogant, for the LORD has spoken.
Give glory to the LORD your God before He brings darkness, before your feet stumble on the dusky mountains. You wait for light, but He turns it into deep gloom and thick darkness.
But if you do not listen, I will weep in secret because of your pride. My eyes will overflow with tears, because the LORD’s flock has been taken captive.
Say to the king and to the queen mother: “Take a lowly seat, for your glorious crowns have fallen from your heads.”
The cities of the Negev have been shut tight, and no one can open them. All Judah has been carried into exile, wholly taken captive.

units.label: Can the leopard change its spots?

verses.text: Lift up your eyes and see those coming from the north. Where is the flock entrusted to you, the sheep that were your pride?
What will you say when He sets over you close allies whom you yourself trained? Will not pangs of anguish grip you, as they do a woman in labor?

And if you ask yourself, “Why has this happened to me?” It is because of the magnitude of your iniquity that your skirts have been stripped off 
and your body has been exposed.
Can the Ethiopian change his skin, or the leopard his {{chabarburah|spots}}? Neither are you able to do good, you who are accustomed to doing evil.
“I will scatter you like chaff driven by the desert wind.
This is your lot, the portion I have measured to you,” declares the LORD, “because you have forgotten Me and trusted in falsehood.
So I will pull your skirts up over your face, that your shame may be seen.
Your adulteries and lustful neighings, your shameless prostitution on the hills and in the fields, I have seen your detestable acts. Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will you remain unclean?”

ezor.gloss: A loincloth or waistband, often a finely made garment worn close to the body. Its intimate closeness to the wearer becomes the sign act's central point, picturing how closely God intended Israel to cling to Him.

davaq.gloss: To cling, cleave, or stay closely attached. This is the same verb used of a husband clinging to his wife in Genesis 2:24, showing God intended a marriage-like closeness with His people, the very closeness pride and idolatry had ruined.

gaon.gloss: Pride, majesty, or exaltation, capable of positive or negative use depending on its object. Here it names the self-exalting pride God will ruin, exactly as the loincloth was ruined, since Judah's pride had displaced its intended closeness to God.

chabarburah.gloss: Spots or markings, used of a leopard's fixed, natural pattern. The rhetorical question exposes that Judah's habitual evil has become as fixed as an animal's markings, requiring God's own transforming work, not mere willpower, to change.

basic.context: God sends Jeremiah on two strange errands. First, he buys a fine linen loincloth, wears it, then buries it by Perath until it rots, useless, a picture of how Judah, meant to cling to the LORD as closely and beautifully as that garment clung to Jeremiah's waist, has ruined that closeness through pride. Second, wineskins will be smashed together in coming judgment. The chapter ends with Jeremiah's own tears and a devastating question: can a leopard change its spots? Judah's sin has become second nature, and only God can address what habit cannot undo.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic street theatre, an enacted parable meant to be seen and remembered, not merely heard, in a culture where visual sign acts carried as much authority as spoken oracles. The rhetorical question about the Ethiopian and the leopard is not a claim that people never change, but an exposure that such change cannot come from the self alone; it requires God's own transforming work.
basic.originalLanguages: The loincloth, ezor, was meant to davaq, cling, to a man's body exactly as Israel was meant to cling to the LORD, the same verb used of a husband clinging to his wife in Genesis 2:24. Judah's gaon, pride, is what God says He will ruin like that spoiled garment. And the piercing question about the leopard's chabarburah, spots, exposes that Judah's evil has become as fixed as an animal's markings, requiring not mere willpower but a new heart.
basic.typology: The loincloth meant to cling closely to Jeremiah pictures the intimate covenant bond God intended with His people, the same clinging language used of marriage in Genesis 2:24, later applied to Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31-32. The impossibility of self-reform, a leopard cannot change its spots, anticipates the New Testament's insistence that only a new birth, not self-improvement, can change the human heart, John 3:3.
basic.christ: The intimacy God intended, Israel clinging to Him as closely as a garment to the waist, for His renown and praise and glory, finds its fulfillment in the union between Christ and His church, described in the same terms of one flesh closeness, Ephesians 5:31-32. Where Jeremiah exposes that habitual sin cannot be undone by willpower alone, the gospel answers with what the law could not do, God sending His own Son to give a new heart and new birth, Romans 8:3, John 3:3.
basic.god: God intended nothing less than the closest possible intimacy with His people, clinging as a loincloth clings to the waist, all for His own renown, praise, and glory. His judgment on pride is real and severe, yet even His most piercing rhetorical question, can the leopard change its spots, is not spoken to crush hope but to expose the depth of a problem only He can solve.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2:24 uses the same verb, cling, for the closeness of marriage that God intended with His people. Ephesians 5:31-32 applies this one flesh closeness to Christ and the church. John 3:3 insists that only a new birth, not self-effort, can change a person. Romans 8:3 explains that God did what the law, and human willpower, could never do.
basic.oneStory: This is the exposure that sin runs deeper than willpower can reach, pictured in a ruined garment and an unchangeable leopard, yet it sits within a story that always intended the closest possible intimacy between God and His people, an intimacy sin could ruin but could not, in the end, defeat, since Christ secures the new birth and new heart no self-effort could ever produce.

context.literary: This chapter continues the ten chapter block of narrative and confession, 11:1-20:18, moving from Jeremiah's own persecution to two enacted sign acts, a favored prophetic form that dramatizes the spoken word.
context.historical: Likely from the reign of Jehoiakim or shortly before, when Judah's political alliances and internal pride blinded the nation's leaders, including the king and queen mother, to the approaching disaster from the north.
context.canonical: This chapter's sign acts join a wider biblical pattern of prophetic enacted parables, alongside Ezekiel's similar dramatized oracles, and its closing image of unchangeable habit anticipates the New Testament's teaching on the necessity of a new birth.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To dramatize, through two vivid enacted parables, how thoroughly Judah's pride has ruined its intended closeness to God, and to press home, through the unanswerable question about the leopard's spots, that the nation's sin has become too deeply ingrained for self-reform.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The sign acts describe a specific prophetic assignment and a specific historical judgment on Judah; they are not a prescription for how believers today should enact symbolic actions. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that pride ruins intimacy with God, and that habitual sin cannot be overcome by human effort alone.

debates.question: Does Jeremiah's question, can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard its spots, teach that people are incapable of any moral change?

views.label: A statement about the impossibility of self-generated change
views.case: The rhetorical question specifically targets people accustomed to doing evil, arguing that ingrained, habitual sin cannot be reformed by mere willpower, a point consistent with the rest of Scripture's insistence on the need for a new heart, not a denial that God can change anyone.

views.label: A rhetorical exaggeration meant to provoke urgency
views.case: Others read the vivid, even startling comparison as deliberately provocative speech, meant to jolt a complacent audience into recognizing the severity of their condition rather than as a literal theological claim about human nature.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the verse exposes that Judah cannot fix itself by trying harder; the wider testimony of Scripture, and this book's own later promise of a new covenant written on the heart, shows that lasting change comes only from God's transforming work, not self-effort.

study.originalLanguages: The loincloth, ezor, was meant to davaq, cling, to a man's body exactly as Israel was meant to cling to the LORD, the same verb used of a husband clinging to his wife in Genesis 2:24. Judah's gaon, pride, is what God says He will ruin like that spoiled garment. And the piercing question about the leopard's chabarburah, spots, exposes that Judah's evil has become as fixed and unchangeable as an animal's natural markings, requiring not mere effort but God's own transforming work.

typology.type: The loincloth meant to cling closely to its wearer, Jeremiah 13:11
typology.fulfillment: Pictures the intimate covenant closeness fulfilled in the union of Christ and His church, described in the same one flesh language, Ephesians 5:31-32.

typology.type: The impossibility of self-reform, a leopard cannot change its spots, Jeremiah 13:23
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's teaching that only a new birth, not self-improvement, can truly change the human heart, John 3:3.

study.christ: The intimacy God intended, Israel clinging to Him as closely as a garment to the waist, for His renown and praise and glory, finds its fulfillment in the union between Christ and His church, described in the same terms of one flesh closeness, Ephesians 5:31-32. Where Jeremiah exposes that habitual sin cannot be undone by willpower alone, can the leopard change its spots, the gospel answers with what the law could never do: God sending His own Son so that sinners might be born again and given a new heart, Romans 8:3, John 3:3.
study.god: God intended nothing less than the closest possible intimacy with His people, clinging as a loincloth clings to the waist, all for His own renown, praise, and glory. His judgment on pride is real and severe enough to smash kings, priests, and prophets together like wineskins, yet even His most piercing question, can the leopard change its spots, is not spoken to crush hope but to expose a depth of need only His own transforming grace can meet.

crossReferences.note: Uses the same verb, cling, for the closeness of marriage that God intended with His people.
Applies this one flesh closeness to the union of Christ and His church.
Insists that only a new birth, not self-effort, can truly change a person.
Explains that God accomplished what the law, and human willpower, could never do.

study.oneStory: This is the exposure that sin runs deeper than willpower can reach, pictured in a ruined garment and an unchangeable leopard, yet it sits within a story that always intended the closest possible intimacy between God and His people, an intimacy sin could ruin but could not, in the end, defeat, since Christ secures the new birth and new heart no self-effort could ever produce.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle, Part 3
sermon.why: Carson expounds the linen girdle and the smashed wine jars of Jeremiah 13 as acted parables of Judah's ruined pride, directly the images and message this study walks through.

quiz.q: What happens to the loincloth Jeremiah buries at Perath?

options.text: It is ruined, of no use at all, after being buried many days.
options.note: Verse 7 states this exactly.

options.text: It remains perfectly preserved despite being buried.
options.note: This directly contradicts verse 7, which describes it as ruined.

options.text: It is stolen by a passing traveler.
options.note: Not described anywhere in the passage.

options.text: It becomes more valuable with age, like fine wine.
options.note: The opposite happens; the garment is spoiled, not enhanced.

quiz.q: What does the ruined loincloth represent, according to 13:9-11?

options.text: The pride of Judah and Jerusalem, ruined because Israel refused to cling to the LORD as intended.
options.note: Verses 9-11 explain this meaning directly.

options.text: God's own faithfulness beginning to fail.
options.note: The opposite; the sign pictures Judah's ruin, not any failure in God's faithfulness.

options.text: The wealth of surrounding foreign nations.
options.note: Not the meaning given in the text; the sign concerns Judah specifically.

options.text: The coming destruction of the temple building itself.
options.note: The sign concerns Judah's pride and closeness to God broadly, not the temple building specifically.

quiz.q: What image does God use in 13:12-14 for the coming judgment?

options.text: Every wineskin filled with wine, then everyone, including kings and priests, smashed against one another in drunkenness.
options.note: Verses 12-14 use this exact image.

options.text: A city rebuilt after its earlier destruction.
options.note: This contradicts the judgment imagery of the passage, which pictures ruin, not rebuilding.

options.text: A flock scattered across distant mountains.
options.note: This image appears elsewhere in Jeremiah, but not in this specific unit.

options.text: A field left fallow for seven years.
options.note: Not the image used in this passage; the specific picture is of wineskins and drunkenness.

quiz.q: According to 13:18, who besides the general population is told to take a lowly seat?

options.text: The king and the queen mother, because their glorious crowns have fallen from their heads.
options.note: Verse 18 addresses these specific figures.

options.text: The prophets who opposed Jeremiah's message.
options.note: Not the specific addressees named in verse 18.

options.text: Foreign ambassadors visiting Jerusalem.
options.note: Not mentioned anywhere in this verse.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: Not the specific figures named in verse 18; the king and queen mother are addressed directly.

quiz.q: What does the question about the Ethiopian's skin and the leopard's spots in 13:23 illustrate?

options.text: That Judah's habitual evil has become so ingrained that they cannot simply reform themselves by effort alone.
options.note: Verse 23 draws this exact conclusion, neither are you able to do good.

options.text: That people can easily change their behavior through willpower alone.
options.note: This directly contradicts the point of the rhetorical question.

options.text: That only foreign nations are capable of deep, ingrained sin.
options.note: The passage applies this point directly to Judah, not exclusively to foreign nations.

options.text: That God unfairly judges people for features they cannot help.
options.note: This misreads the analogy, which concerns ingrained moral habit, not an unfair judgment based on physical features.

questions: The loincloth was meant to cling closely to Jeremiah, just as Israel was meant to cling to the LORD. What competes for the place of closeness God intends to have with you?
Judah's pride is pictured as a ruined, useless garment. Where might pride be quietly ruining something valuable in your own life or relationships?
Even the king and queen mother were told to take a lowly seat before God. What position of pride or status might you need to lay down before Him?

The question, can a leopard change its spots, exposes that some patterns of sin run deeper than willpower can fix. Where have you been trying to change something in your own strength that actually needs God's transforming work?
God intended Israel's closeness to Him to be for His own renown, praise, and glory. How does knowing that your closeness to God brings Him praise change how you approach Him today?

translationNotes.note: Perath most likely refers to the Euphrates river, though some propose a nearer wadi or village of similar name; either reading preserves the symbolic distance and difficulty of the journey involved in the sign act.
The queen mother held a position of real political influence in Judah's royal court, so her humbling alongside the king signals total, not merely symbolic, national disgrace.
Ethiopian translates a word referring to the Cushite region south of Egypt; the illustration concerns unchangeable, natural features, not any moral judgment on that people themselves.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web fetch this session confirming Don Carson's sermon covering Jeremiah 11 to 15 at thegospelcoalition.org.
